introduction.subtitle: How do you decide under uncertainty? Five axes of your decision reflex.
introduction.description:
This is not a test of decision quality — it maps your style. Each pole of each axis has a use; what matters is knowing your reflex so you can tell when it serves the situation and when it does not.

Each scenario offers a clear-leaning option, a balanced option, and an opposite-leaning option. Pick what you would actually do.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is your choice.
introduction.disclaimer: Original instrument. This is not a validated psychometric assessment.

questions.text: Two potential customers: one large contract needing heavy customization, one small but perfectly aligned with your roadmap. One week to decide. What do you do?

options.text: Calculate revenue, cost-to-serve, and strategic fit before deciding.
Trust your gut about which customer fits.
Sketch the numbers fast, then check against instinct.

questions.text: A senior teammate challenges your plan with a strong objection. How do you respond?

options.text: Ask for their reasoning and weigh the evidence point by point.
Sense whether the objection carries real signal beyond the logic.
Hear the point, then weigh both the reasoning and your instincts.

questions.text: A new market looks promising but the data is thin. What is your first move?

options.text: Commission research before committing anything.
Act on pattern recognition from analogous markets.
Run one cheap experiment to triangulate.

questions.text: A partnership could double revenue but requires sharing your core technology. The partner is reputable; the agreement is complex. What do you do?

options.text: Take it — the upside justifies sharing the technology.
Decline — protect the core.
Negotiate protections first, then decide.

questions.text: A bet-the-company opportunity with roughly 50/50 odds appears. How do you handle it?

options.text: Take the bet — the upside is transformative.
Preserve optionality instead of going all-in.
Take a smaller, survivable version of the bet.

questions.text: Cash reserves could fund a risky expansion or simply extend runway. Which do you favor?

options.text: Expand — growth is what protects you long term.
Extend runway first — survive before you grow.
Split the difference: a measured expansion and a longer buffer.

questions.text: You need to hire in two weeks. Candidates: clearly qualified but expensive, promising but unproven, or safe but dull. What do you do?

options.text: Decide fast on first impression; iterate if wrong.
Interview thoroughly and check references before deciding.
Add one extra round, then decide.

questions.text: A competitor ships a feature close to yours. How do you respond?

options.text: Ship something now, iterate from real feedback.
Get it right; ship in a month.
Ship a minimal version now, polish after.

questions.text: A critical bug appears at launch. What is your first move?

options.text: Patch fast, communicate after.
Pause and diagnose the root cause first.
Quick containment, then root-cause it.

questions.text: A key supplier fails mid-project. What do you do?

options.text: Direct the replacement personally and closely.
Let the team reorganize around what is available.
Set the constraint, let them find the path.

questions.text: A funder wants real influence over your roadmap. How do you respond?

options.text: Keep direction firmly with the founders.
Adapt strategy to their input where it genuinely improves it.
Hold the core, flex the edges.

questions.text: The market shifts while you are still building. What do you do?

options.text: Stick to the plan; you chose it for a reason.
Re-shape around the new reality.
Re-plan the next milestone, keep the vision.

questions.text: A hard strategic call has to be made. How do you make it?

options.text: Decide it yourself — you carry the risk.
Build consensus with the team first.
Consult two trusted people, then decide.

questions.text: A crisis hits at 2 a.m. What do you do?

options.text: Handle it yourself, brief others after.
Wake the people who can help.
Make the urgent call, loop others in for the rest.

questions.text: You are setting the company’s direction. How do you shape it?

options.text: Keep it yours — you carry the risk.
Co-author it with the team.
Draft it, then pressure-test with a few.
